<wp_judge> Evaluate this WordPress code:

```php
function lp_register_routes() {
    register_rest_route( 'lp/v1', '/items', array(
        'methods'  => 'GET',
        'callback' => 'lp_get_items',
    ) );
    foreach ( $routes as $r ) {
        do_action( 'lp_route', $r );

```

lp_register_routes() is missing its closing brace and references an undefined $routes variable, so the file does not parse.

WPCS Compliance: score 2/10 — Unparseable code cannot satisfy WPCS; the unterminated block is a hard error.

SQL Safety: score None/10 — No $wpdb usage or SQL strings — N/A applies.

Security: score 6/10 — No direct user-input handling in this snippet; no specific injection sink observed.

Performance: score 8/10 — Trivial control flow; performance is not a concern here.

WP API Usage: score 5/10 — Minimal WordPress API integration shown in the snippet.

Code Quality: score 1/10 — The function body is missing its closing brace and `$routes` is never defined — this does not parse; PHP raises a fatal `syntax error, unexpected end of file`.

Dependency Integrity: score 6/10 — No external dependencies beyond core are referenced.

i18n: score None/10 — No user-facing translatable strings — N/A applies.

Accessibility: score 3/10 — No HTML output; accessibility is not applicable to this code.

[/REASONING]

<wp_judge> Evaluate this WordPress code:

```php
function get_site_slug() {
    $slug = sanitize_title( get_bloginfo( 'name' ) )
    return $slug;
}
```

get_site_slug() omits the semicolon after the sanitize_title() assignment, producing a fatal parse error before the return.

WPCS Compliance: score 3/10 — A parse error precludes any standards compliance.

SQL Safety: score None/10 — No $wpdb usage or SQL strings — N/A applies.

Security: score 6/10 — No direct user-input handling in this snippet; no specific injection sink observed.

Performance: score 8/10 — Trivial control flow; performance is not a concern here.

WP API Usage: score 5/10 — Minimal WordPress API integration shown in the snippet.

Code Quality: score 1/10 — Missing semicolon after the `sanitize_title(...)` assignment — PHP raises `syntax error, unexpected 'return'`. The function never parses.

Dependency Integrity: score 6/10 — No external dependencies beyond core are referenced.

i18n: score None/10 — No user-facing translatable strings — N/A applies.

Accessibility: score 3/10 — No HTML output; accessibility is not applicable to this code.

[/REASONING]

Evaluate this WordPress code: ```php function render_box() { $html = <<Hello return $html; } ```

render_box() opens a heredoc that is never terminated, so the parser consumes the rest of the function as string content — a fatal error.

WPCS Compliance: score 4/10 — No PHPDoc block and the snippet shows formatting/style gaps; below WPCS expectations.

SQL Safety: score None/10 — No $wpdb usage or SQL strings — N/A applies.

Security: score 4/10 — Even if it parsed, the heredoc emits markup without escaping.

Performance: score 8/10 — Trivial control flow; performance is not a concern here.

WP API Usage: score 5/10 — Minimal WordPress API integration shown in the snippet.

Code Quality: score 1/10 — The heredoc opened with `<<<HTML` is never closed by a `HTML;` terminator at column 0 — fatal parse error; everything after is swallowed into the string.

Dependency Integrity: score 6/10 — No external dependencies beyond core are referenced.

i18n: score None/10 — No user-facing translatable strings — N/A applies.

Accessibility: score 3/10 — No HTML output; accessibility is not applicable to this code.

[/REASONING]

<wp_judge> Evaluate this WordPress code:

```php
add_filter( 'body_class', function( $classes ) {
    $classes[] = 'custom';
    return $classes;
);
```

The body_class closure is missing its closing brace before );, leaving brackets unbalanced and the file unparseable.

<wp_judge> Evaluate this WordPress code:

```php
function list_items() {
    global $wpdb;
    $col = $_GET['sort'];
    return $wpdb->get_results( "SELECT * FROM {$wpdb->posts} ORDER BY $col" );
}
```

list_items() concatenates $_GET['sort'] into an ORDER BY clause, a SQL injection with no allowlist or prepare.

WPCS Compliance: score 4/10 — No PHPDoc block and the snippet shows formatting/style gaps; below WPCS expectations.

SQL Safety: score 1/10 — `$_GET['sort']` is concatenated straight into the ORDER BY clause with no allowlist or `$wpdb->prepare()` — a textbook SQL injection. ORDER BY cannot be parameterized, so it must be validated against a fixed column allowlist.

Security: score 1/10 — Unauthenticated SQL injection sink.

Performance: score 8/10 — Trivial control flow; performance is not a concern here.

WP API Usage: score 5/10 — Minimal WordPress API integration shown in the snippet.

Code Quality: score 4/10 — No input validation at all.

Dependency Integrity: score 6/10 — No external dependencies beyond core are referenced.

i18n: score None/10 — No user-facing translatable strings — N/A applies.

Accessibility: score 3/10 — No HTML output; accessibility is not applicable to this code.

[/REASONING]

<wp_judge> Evaluate this WordPress code:

```php
function posts_in() {
    global $wpdb;
    $ids = $_POST['ids'];
    return $wpdb->get_results( "SELECT * FROM {$wpdb->posts} WHERE ID IN ($ids)" );
}
```

posts_in() interpolates $_POST['ids'] into an IN() list unprepared — SQL injection.

WPCS Compliance: score 4/10 — No PHPDoc block and the snippet shows formatting/style gaps; below WPCS expectations.

SQL Safety: score 1/10 — `$_POST['ids']` is interpolated directly into the IN() list — SQL injection. The safe pattern builds placeholders and uses `$wpdb->prepare()` with the id array.

Security: score 1/10 — Direct injection from request body.

Performance: score 8/10 — Trivial control flow; performance is not a concern here.

WP API Usage: score 5/10 — Minimal WordPress API integration shown in the snippet.

Code Quality: score 4/10 — No sanitization or integer casting of ids.
